title: Sysmex UF-1000i Urine Analyzer - Sample Aspiration / Probe Clog
description: Troubleshooting UF-1000i sample aspiration errors, probe clog warnings, low sample flow, or specimen pickup issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the UF-1000i is not actively processing patient specimens before opening covers, handling racks, checking sample areas, or initiating cleaning. Expected outcome: No patient specimen is interrupted, lost, or reported from a device with an unresolved aspiration issue.

steps.title: Stop Additional Sample Processing
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff not to load additional racks or specimens until the aspiration or clog issue is evaluated. Confirm whether any specimens were in process when the error occurred and whether results were completed, flagged, rejected, or require repeat testing per laboratory policy. Expected outcome: Incomplete or questionable urine results are not released.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue involves: Sample aspiration error; Probe clog message; Low sample volume; Missed specimen pickup; Air bubbles in the sample path; Repeated failed samples; Abnormal scattergram or count results following aspiration; Probe not descending or returning properly. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to sample pickup, probe movement, specimen condition, or fluid path behavior.

steps.title: Check the Specimen Cup and Sample Volume
steps.instructions: Verify the urine specimen cup is properly filled, correctly positioned, and not underfilled. Check for foam, heavy sediment, mucus, clots, crystals, or debris that may interfere with aspiration. Expected outcome: A low-volume or obstructed specimen is identified before assuming analyzer failure. If replacing or remixed specimen handling resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the finding.

steps.title: Verify Rack and Tube/Cup Positioning
steps.instructions: Confirm the rack is the correct type, seated properly, and not damaged. Check that specimen containers are sitting fully in the rack and are not tilted, cracked, overfilled, or placed in the wrong position. Expected outcome: The probe can align with the specimen container without obstruction or mispositioning.

steps.title: Inspect the Sample Probe Area Externally
steps.instructions: With the device safely paused and access permitted, visually inspect the sample probe area for visible dried urine, crystals, residue, or obstruction. Do not bend, force, scrape, or manually realign the probe. Expected outcome: Any obvious external buildup or obstruction is identified without damaging the probe.

steps.title: Check for Visible Probe Damage
steps.instructions: Look for obvious signs of a bent, loose, cracked, or contaminated probe. If the probe appears bent or physically damaged, remove the analyzer from service and escalate to repair. Expected outcome: Physical probe damage is recognized as a repair issue, not a cleaning issue.

steps.title: Run the User-Accessible Cleaning or Rinse Function
steps.instructions: If available to Clinical Engineering and permitted by facility procedure, run the analyzer’s normal rinse, cleaning, or probe maintenance cycle. Use only approved cleaning solutions and laboratory-approved maintenance materials. Expected outcome: Minor residue, urine crystals, or partial blockage may clear through normal maintenance. If aspiration returns to normal after cleaning, stop troubleshooting and document the cleaning action and successful test.

steps.title: Check Reagent, Sheath, Diluent, and Waste Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify required fluids are present, connected, not expired where applicable, and not empty. Confirm waste containers are not full and that waste tubing is not kinked, pinched, or disconnected. Expected outcome: Aspiration errors are not being caused by downstream fluid handling or pressure problems.

steps.title: Inspect External Tubing and Fluid Path Connections
steps.instructions: Check visible, user-accessible tubing for kinks, air gaps, loose fittings, leaks, crystallized residue, or pinch points. Do not disconnect internal tubing unless authorized by service procedure and local policy. Expected outcome: External fluid restrictions or leaks are ruled out.

steps.title: Listen for Abnormal Pump or Aspiration Sounds
steps.instructions: During a safe test or maintenance cycle, listen for unusual clicking, grinding, repeated priming, weak suction, or pump strain. Expected outcome: Abnormal pump behavior may indicate an internal fluidics or drive issue requiring bench evaluation.

steps.title: Check Error Timing
steps.instructions: Determine when the error occurs: Before probe descent; During specimen pickup; During rinse or wash; After aspiration; Only on certain racks or specimens; On every sample. Expected outcome: Repeated failure on all samples suggests an analyzer issue, while failure on certain specimens may point to container, volume, or sample condition.

steps.title: Try a Known Good Control or Approved Test Specimen
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff run an approved control, maintenance sample, or known acceptable specimen according to laboratory policy. Do not use patient testing as a trial if the analyzer’s reliability is questionable. Expected outcome: A known good sample helps separate analyzer aspiration failure from poor specimen quality.

steps.title: Check for Related QC or Scattergram Abnormalities
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether QC failed, scattergrams looked abnormal, counts were unexpectedly low, or samples required frequent repeat testing before the clog error appeared. Expected outcome: Trending quality issues may confirm a developing fluidic restriction rather than a single specimen problem.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the analyzer is idle and results are protected, perform a normal software shutdown and restart if local procedure allows. Do not power cycle during active sample processing. Expected outcome: A temporary software or sensor state may clear, but repeated aspiration errors still require further evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Operation After Corrective Action
steps.instructions: After any cleaning, fluid correction, rack correction, or restart, have laboratory staff run QC or an approved verification process before returning the UF-1000i to patient testing. Expected outcome: The analyzer is not returned to service until acceptable operation is confirmed.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported the Sysmex UF-1000i displayed sample aspiration errors and failed to process several urine specimens.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found adequate reagent levels but visible residue near the sample probe area and repeated aspiration failure during verification.
CCR.Resolution: Ran approved probe cleaning/rinse cycle, verified rack and sample cup positioning, had lab perform QC verification, and returned analyzer to service after successful operation.

helpfulDetails: Error message or code displayed
Whether the issue affected one specimen or all specimens
Sample volume and specimen condition
Rack type and rack position involved
Probe condition observed externally
Reagent, sheath, diluent, and waste status
Tubing checked for kinks or leaks
Cleaning or rinse cycle performed
QC or verification result after troubleshooting
Any abnormal pump sounds
Presence of air bubbles, leaks, residue, unusual odor, heat, or fluid buildup
Final device status

title: Sysmex UF-1000i Urine Analyzer - Sheath Fluid / Reagent / Waste Error
description: Troubleshooting UF-1000i sheath fluid, reagent, waste level, container, tubing, and fluid management errors before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the UF-1000i is not actively processing patient specimens before opening fluid areas, replacing containers, moving waste lines, or restarting the analyzer. If urine testing must continue, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime process. Expected outcome: No patient specimen is interrupted, delayed, or resulted from a device with unresolved fluid handling issues.

steps.title: Stop Additional Sample Processing
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff not to load additional specimens until the sheath fluid, reagent, or waste error is cleared. Confirm whether any specimens were in process when the error occurred and whether results were completed, flagged, aborted, or need repeat testing per lab policy. Expected outcome: Incomplete or questionable urine results are not released.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Fluid Error
steps.instructions: Review the analyzer screen, IPU/workstation message, and staff report. Determine whether the issue involves: Sheath fluid empty or low; Reagent empty or low; Waste full; Waste container not detected; Fluid replacement error; Bubble or flow-related message; Reagent pack or container recognition issue; Repeated fluid errors after replacement. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to supply level, waste level, container setup, tubing, detection, or internal fluidics.

steps.title: Check Sheath Fluid Level and Container Placement
steps.instructions: Verify the sheath fluid container has adequate volume and is properly seated in its assigned location. Confirm the container is not expired, empty, damaged, collapsed, or incorrectly installed. Expected outcome: A low or empty sheath fluid condition is corrected. If the error clears, document the action and stop.

steps.title: Check Reagent Level and Correct Reagent Type
steps.instructions: Confirm the correct Sysmex-compatible reagent is installed and that the bottle, pack, or container matches the analyzer requirement. Look for expired reagent, improper storage, empty container, loose cap, damaged connector, or incorrect bottle position. Expected outcome: Incorrect, empty, expired, or mispositioned reagent is identified and corrected. If resolved, return the analyzer to service after lab verification.

steps.title: Inspect Waste Container Level
steps.instructions: Verify the waste container is not full or near full. Empty or replace the waste container according to laboratory safety procedure and biohazard handling policy. Confirm the cap and tubing are reconnected securely afterward. Expected outcome: A full waste condition is corrected and the analyzer can resume normal operation.

steps.title: Confirm Waste Container Is Properly Seated
steps.instructions: Check that the waste container is in the correct location and that any sensor, cap, float, or detection connection is properly positioned. A container that is slightly misaligned can trigger a waste error even when it is not full. Expected outcome: Waste detection returns to normal. If the message clears, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect External Fluid Tubing
steps.instructions: Visually inspect user-accessible sheath, reagent, and waste tubing. Look for: Kinks; Pinches; Loose fittings; Cracked tubing; Disconnected lines; Fluid leakage; Air bubbles; Tubing routed under the analyzer or container. Expected outcome: External tubing restrictions or disconnections are corrected before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Check for Leaks or Fluid Residue
steps.instructions: Inspect around the analyzer base, reagent area, waste container, and tubing connections for fluid residue, wet surfaces, crystallized deposits, or odor. Do not continue operation if active leakage is present. Expected outcome: Any leak is identified and the device is removed from service if fluid containment is compromised.

steps.title: Verify Container Caps and Pickup Tubes
steps.instructions: Confirm pickup tubes are inserted fully into the correct containers and are not sitting above the fluid level. Make sure caps are not cross-threaded and that tubing is not swapped between sheath, reagent, and waste positions. Expected outcome: The analyzer can draw fluid and drain waste through the correct external paths.

steps.title: Check Recent Fluid Replacement History
steps.instructions: Ask lab staff whether sheath fluid, reagent, or waste was replaced shortly before the error appeared. Confirm whether the correct replacement sequence was followed and whether the analyzer was allowed to recognize the new container. Expected outcome: Errors caused by recent container replacement, loose tubing, or missed confirmation steps are identified.

steps.title: Run the Appropriate User-Level Prime, Rinse, or Fluid Initialization Function
steps.instructions: If allowed by site procedure and operator access, have qualified lab staff initiate the appropriate user-level fluid replacement, prime, rinse, or initialization function from the analyzer or IPU. Do not bypass alarms or repeatedly force operation if fluid movement fails. Expected outcome: Air is cleared from the line and the analyzer recognizes normal fluid status.

steps.title: Observe for Recurring Error After Reset
steps.instructions: After correcting external fluid or waste issues, clear the alarm using normal operator workflow and observe whether the same error returns. Do not return the analyzer to service based only on a cleared message if the same error repeats immediately. Expected outcome: A one-time container or tubing issue is separated from a recurring fluidics or sensor problem.

steps.title: Check for Unusual Pump or Valve Behavior
steps.instructions: Listen for abnormal sounds during user-level fluid priming or startup, such as repeated clicking, no pump sound, strain, grinding, or prolonged cycling. Do not open internal covers or attempt board-level or pump repair during field troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Abnormal internal fluid movement is recognized as a repair-level issue.

steps.title: Confirm Analyzer Status With Laboratory Staff
steps.instructions: Have lab staff confirm whether the analyzer passes its required startup checks, background checks, QC, or site-required verification after the fluid issue is corrected. Expected outcome: The UF-1000i is only returned to service after the lab confirms acceptable operation.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported the Sysmex UF-1000i displayed a sheath fluid/reagent/waste error and stopped urine sample processing.
CCR.Cause: Found the waste container full and the waste tubing slightly kinked behind the analyzer, preventing normal waste drainage.
CCR.Resolution: Emptied the waste container per lab biohazard procedure, corrected tubing routing, cleared the error, and had lab staff verify analyzer operation with required checks.

helpfulDetails: Exact fluid or waste error message displayed
Whether sheath fluid was low, empty, expired, or recently replaced
Reagent lot, expiration, and correct container placement
Waste container level and condition
Whether waste tubing was kinked, blocked, or disconnected
Any visible leaks, residue, odor, or fluid under the analyzer
Whether air bubbles were seen in external tubing
Whether user-level prime, rinse, or fluid initialization was performed
Any abnormal pump, valve, or clicking sounds
Whether startup checks, background checks, or QC passed afterward
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Sysmex UF-1000i Urine Analyzer - Flow Cell Contamination / Background Count Issue
description: Troubleshooting elevated background counts, possible flow cell contamination, debris, carryover, or abnormal particle counts on the Sysmex UF-1000i.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Confirm the UF-1000i is not actively processing patient specimens before stopping operation, running cleaning, checking reagents, or inspecting fluid areas.

Expected outcome: No patient specimen is interrupted, lost, or resulted from a device with a suspected contamination issue.

steps.title: Stop Reporting Questionable Results
steps.instructions:
Ask laboratory staff whether abnormal background counts, unexpected particles, or carryover concerns affected patient specimens.

Confirm whether affected results were held, repeated, reviewed, or verified according to laboratory policy.

Expected outcome: Questionable urine particle results are not released without appropriate review.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Background Complaint
steps.instructions:
Ask staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue involves:

Elevated background count
Flow cell contamination message
Abnormal scattergram background
Debris or particles with blank/sample checks
Failed background check
Carryover after abnormal specimens
QC failure related to elevated counts
Repeated rinse or cleaning prompts

Expected outcome: The issue is separated into contamination, carryover, reagent, QC, or sample-related concerns.

steps.title: Check for Recent Problem Specimens
steps.instructions:
Ask whether the issue began after highly turbid urine, heavy sediment, blood, mucus, bacteria, crystals, or unusually concentrated specimens.

If so, have the lab follow their approved repeat, dilution, manual review, or rerun policy.

Expected outcome: A difficult specimen is identified as a possible source of contamination or carryover.

steps.title: Verify Reagent and Sheath Fluid Status
steps.instructions:
Check that all required Sysmex-approved fluids are present, properly installed, not expired, and not empty.

Confirm containers are not collapsed, contaminated, incorrectly connected, or left open.

Expected outcome: Background issues are not being caused by improper fluid supply or contaminated reagent handling.

steps.title: Check Waste Container or Drain Condition
steps.instructions:
Confirm the waste container is not full and that the waste line is not kinked, pinched, blocked, or improperly positioned.

Poor waste removal can affect fluid movement and rinse effectiveness.

Expected outcome: Waste can drain normally during rinse and cleaning cycles.

steps.title: Inspect External Tubing and Fluid Lines
steps.instructions:
Visually inspect user-accessible tubing for air bubbles, kinks, dried residue, loose fittings, leaks, or discoloration.

Do not disconnect internal tubing unless trained and authorized.

Expected outcome: External fluid path issues are either corrected or ruled out.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Sample Tube and Rack Handling
steps.instructions:
Check whether sample tubes are compatible, filled appropriately, mixed correctly, and seated properly in the rack.

Poor sample preparation can introduce clots, sediment buildup, foam, or carryover risk.

Expected outcome: Sample handling problems are ruled out before assuming analyzer failure.

steps.title: Review Error History and Background Trend
steps.instructions:
Check the analyzer or IPU/workstation for recent background errors, clog warnings, rinse failures, reagent warnings, or QC failures.

Look for repeated events rather than a single isolated failure.

Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as recurring, intermittent, or isolated.

steps.title: Run the Operator-Accessible Rinse or Background Check
steps.instructions:
Use the normal user-accessible maintenance or background check process approved by the laboratory and facility procedure.

Do not bypass required quality checks.

Expected outcome: Background count returns to acceptable range, or the failure repeats.

If this resolves the issue, document the cleaning/rinse action and stop.

steps.title: Run Approved Cleaning Maintenance
steps.instructions:
If the background remains elevated, have authorized staff perform the approved cleaning cycle using the correct Sysmex cleaning solution and procedure.

Avoid improvised cleaners or unapproved fluids, as they may damage the flow cell or fluid path.

Expected outcome: Contamination clears and background count returns to acceptable limits.

If this resolves the issue, confirm acceptable QC or background results before returning the analyzer to service.

steps.title: Check for Carryover After Cleaning
steps.instructions:
After cleaning, confirm with the laboratory that background checks, blanks, QC, or repeat samples are acceptable according to their policy.

Expected outcome: The analyzer demonstrates stable background performance before patient testing resumes.

steps.title: Verify QC Performance
steps.instructions:
Ask the lab to confirm QC acceptability after cleaning and background correction.

If QC remains abnormal despite acceptable external checks and cleaning, do not return the unit to routine use.

Expected outcome: The device is either verified for use or identified for escalation.

steps.title: Check IPU/Workstation Displayed Flags
steps.instructions:
Review whether the abnormality is being reported by the analyzer, IPU, QC program, or middleware.

This helps separate true analyzer background contamination from software interpretation or result handling issues.

Expected outcome: The source of the background concern is better isolated.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Repeated Cleaning Indefinitely
steps.instructions:
If multiple approved cleanings do not correct the background count, stop troubleshooting.

Repeated cleaning without improvement may delay testing and can mask a deeper fluidic, flow cell, detector, or contamination issue.

Expected outcome: The device is escalated appropriately instead of being repeatedly cycled without resolution.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported elevated background counts and abnormal scattergram background on the Sysmex UF-1000i urine analyzer.
CCR.Cause: External checks found adequate reagent and waste levels, no visible tubing kinks, and persistent elevated background after approved cleaning.
CCR.Resolution: Analyzer was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated for bench evaluation due to suspected internal flow cell or fluidics contamination.

helpfulDetails: Background count values or failure message observed
Whether patient results were affected or held
Whether QC passed or failed
Reagent and sheath fluid status
Waste container or drain condition
Cleaning cycle performed
Number of background checks attempted
Any recent highly turbid or sediment-heavy specimens
Visible bubbles, leaks, residue, or discoloration
Scattergram abnormality observed
Final device status

title: Sysmex UF-1000i Urine Analyzer - QC Failure / Particle Count Abnormality
description: Troubleshooting UF-1000i QC failures, abnormal particle counts, background issues, scattergram concerns, or questionable urine sediment results.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Sysmex UF-1000i is not actively processing patient specimens before troubleshooting QC, background, reagent, sample, or fluidic concerns. If urine testing must continue, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime process. Expected outcome: No patient results are released from a device with unresolved QC or particle count concerns.

steps.title: Stop Reporting Questionable Results
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff whether patient specimens were processed after the QC failure or abnormal particle count issue appeared. Confirm whether affected specimens were held, repeated, manually reviewed, or verified according to laboratory policy. Expected outcome: Potentially inaccurate urine particle results are controlled before release.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact QC or Count Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what failed or looked abnormal. Check whether the issue involves: QC outside acceptable range; Repeated QC failure; High background count; Abnormal RBC, WBC, epithelial cell, bacteria, cast, or crystal counts; Scattergram shift or unusual cluster pattern; Unexpected positive or negative results; Repeatability problems; Flagged or unreliable particle classification. Expected outcome: The problem is narrowed to QC material, sample handling, reagents, background contamination, fluidics, or possible analyzer performance.

steps.title: Check QC Material Handling
steps.instructions: Verify that the correct QC material was used, that it is not expired, and that it was stored and mixed according to laboratory procedure. Confirm that the correct QC level and lot were selected on the analyzer or workstation. Expected outcome: QC failure caused by incorrect, expired, unmixed, or improperly selected QC material is ruled out.

steps.title: Repeat QC With Proper Mixing
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff prepare and repeat QC using a properly mixed sample. Avoid bubbles, foam, sediment settling, or insufficient volume. Expected outcome: If QC passes after proper preparation, document the handling issue and return the analyzer to service per lab policy.

steps.title: Check Recent Reagent or Consumable Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether sheath fluid, diluent, stain, cleaning solution, or other consumables were recently replaced. Verify that containers are installed correctly, not expired, not empty, and not connected to the wrong line. Expected outcome: Reagent-related count abnormalities are identified before assuming analyzer failure.

steps.title: Inspect Waste and Fluid Containers
steps.instructions: Check that waste is not full, tubing is not kinked, and fluid containers are seated securely. Look for low fluid, collapsed tubing, leaking fittings, or air entering the fluid path. Expected outcome: Fluid supply or waste restrictions that could affect particle counting are corrected.

steps.title: Check for Air Bubbles or Flow Interruption
steps.instructions: Observe any user-visible tubing and fluid areas for air bubbles, poor flow, or interrupted reagent movement. Air in the flow path can affect flow stability and cause abnormal counts or QC variation. Expected outcome: Air or flow instability is identified and corrected using approved priming or preparation steps.

steps.title: Run Approved Background or Blank Check
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff run the analyzer’s approved background, blank, or cleaning verification process if available to the user. Expected outcome: Background contamination is either confirmed or ruled out.

steps.title: Perform Approved User-Level Cleaning
steps.instructions: If background is high, particle counts are unstable, or scattergrams appear abnormal, perform only the approved user-level cleaning or rinse process. Do not disassemble the flow cell, detector area, aspiration system, or internal tubing unless trained and authorized. Expected outcome: Contamination or carryover is cleared without unnecessary internal service intervention.

steps.title: Check Sample Probe Area for External Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the accessible sample aspiration area for dried urine, debris, salt buildup, or residue around the probe path. Clean only external, user-accessible surfaces according to facility and manufacturer-approved procedures. Expected outcome: External contamination affecting sample pickup or carryover is removed.

steps.title: Confirm Sample Tube and Rack Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify that QC or specimens are in appropriate tubes, properly filled, correctly positioned, and not obstructed by caps, labels, or rack misalignment. Expected outcome: Improper sample presentation is ruled out as a cause of abnormal particle counts.

steps.title: Review Recent Maintenance History
steps.instructions: Check whether daily, weekly, or scheduled maintenance was missed, delayed, or recently performed incorrectly. Look for repeated QC failures after maintenance, reagent replacement, or cleaning. Expected outcome: The issue is tied to a recent maintenance or setup event if applicable.

steps.title: Check Environmental and Handling Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm that the analyzer is on a stable surface and not exposed to excessive vibration, heat, direct sunlight, or nearby fluid spills. Expected outcome: External environmental causes of unstable readings are ruled out.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If the analyzer is idle and results are protected, perform a controlled restart of the UF-1000i and associated workstation if appropriate. Allow the system to initialize fully before repeating QC or background checks. Expected outcome: Temporary software or initialization faults are cleared.

steps.title: Repeat QC and Compare Results
steps.instructions: After correcting any external issue, repeat QC using fresh, properly prepared QC material. Compare the result pattern to the original failure. Expected outcome: Passing QC supports returning the analyzer to service per lab policy. Repeated failure indicates further evaluation is needed.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Issue Is Isolated or Persistent
steps.instructions: Check whether the abnormality affects one QC level, one parameter, all particle counts, or both QC and patient samples. Expected outcome: The failure pattern helps determine whether the issue is likely material-specific, fluidic, contamination-related, or internal.

steps.title: Do Not Override QC Without Lab Approval
steps.instructions: Clinical Engineering should not advise bypassing QC lockouts, suppressing flags, or releasing results from a failed QC state. Expected outcome: Laboratory quality policy and patient safety remain protected.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported repeated QC failure and abnormal WBC/RBC particle counts on the Sysmex UF-1000i.
CCR.Cause: Verified QC material, reagent levels, waste status, sample positioning, and user-accessible fluid lines; abnormal background count persisted after approved cleaning and repeat QC.
CCR.Resolution: Removed UF-1000i from service, labeled device Out of Service, notified laboratory leadership, and escalated for vendor or bench evaluation.

helpfulDetails: QC level and lot number used
QC expiration date checked
Whether QC was repeated with fresh material
Specific failed parameter, such as RBC, WBC, bacteria, casts, or background
Scattergram behavior or abnormal cluster pattern
Reagent and sheath fluid status
Waste container status
Any air bubbles, leaks, kinks, or visible flow issues
Cleaning or background check performed
Error codes, flags, or alarm messages displayed
Alarm behavior
Accessories swapped
Power behavior
Environmental factors
Indicator lights
Whether patient specimens were affected
Final device status

title: Sysmex UF-1000i Urine Analyzer - IPU / Workstation / LIS Communication Failure
description: Troubleshooting UF-1000i IPU, workstation, host, middleware, or LIS communication issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Confirm the UF-1000i is not actively processing patient specimens before restarting the analyzer, IPU, workstation, middleware interface, or network connection.
If testing must continue, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime process.
Expected outcome: Patient testing and result reporting are protected during communication troubleshooting.

steps.title: Protect Result Integrity
steps.instructions:
Ask laboratory staff whether any specimens were processed while communication was down.
Confirm whether results are stored locally on the IPU, pending transmission, rejected by middleware, or missing from the LIS.
Expected outcome: No urine particle results are released, duplicated, or lost without laboratory review.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication Complaint
steps.instructions:
Determine what is failing. Check whether the issue involves:
Results not transmitting to the LIS
Orders or worklists not downloading
IPU not communicating with the analyzer
Middleware rejection
Host communication error
Network connection failure
Delayed or intermittent result transfer
Results visible on the IPU but not in the LIS
Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to analyzer-to-IPU, IPU-to-middleware, middleware-to-LIS, or network communication.

steps.title: Check for Active Analyzer or IPU Alarms
steps.instructions:
Review the UF-1000i analyzer screen and IPU/workstation for communication alarms, system status messages, or host interface warnings.
Record the exact message before clearing anything.
Expected outcome: The reported issue is matched to a specific communication or interface condition.

steps.title: Verify the Analyzer and IPU Are Powered On and Fully Booted
steps.instructions:
Confirm the UF-1000i analyzer, IPU/workstation, monitor, and any connected interface PC are powered on and not frozen.
Check for normal status indicators and that the operating software is open.
Expected outcome: Communication is not being interrupted by a powered-off, locked, or unresponsive workstation.

steps.title: Check Physical Network and Data Connections
steps.instructions:
Inspect Ethernet, serial, USB, or interface cables used between the analyzer, IPU, middleware, and network.
Make sure cables are fully seated, not damaged, and connected to the correct ports.
Expected outcome: Loose or disconnected communication cabling is ruled out.

steps.title: Check Network Jack and Switch Link Lights
steps.instructions:
Verify the network wall jack and switch port show link/activity lights where applicable.
Compare with a known working nearby network connection if allowed by facility policy.
Expected outcome: A failed network drop, inactive switch port, or disconnected patch cable is identified or ruled out.

steps.title: Confirm the IPU Is on the Network
steps.instructions:
Check whether the IPU/workstation has normal network connectivity according to site policy.
Verify that the network icon does not show disconnected status and that the workstation has the expected IP configuration if accessible.
Expected outcome: The IPU is confirmed to be connected to the hospital or laboratory network.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions:
Ask staff whether anything changed recently, such as:
Network jack move
Workstation replacement
Middleware update
LIS upgrade
IP address change
Analyzer relocation
Power outage
User account or password change
Interface engine maintenance
Expected outcome: The communication failure is linked to a recent environmental, IT, or interface change.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Workflow Path
steps.instructions:
Confirm with laboratory staff whether the UF-1000i sends results directly to the LIS or through middleware.
Check whether other lab instruments using the same middleware are communicating normally.
Expected outcome: The issue is isolated to the UF-1000i/IPU path or identified as a broader middleware/LIS issue.

steps.title: Check IPU Software Status
steps.instructions:
Confirm the UF-1000i IPU application is running, not locked up, and not displaying database, host, or queue errors.
Do not delete result queues unless directed by laboratory policy or vendor support.
Expected outcome: The workstation software is available to receive, store, and transmit results.

steps.title: Check Result Queue or Transmission Status
steps.instructions:
With laboratory involvement, confirm whether results are waiting to transmit, failed, rejected, or already marked as sent.
Record examples of affected sample IDs if permitted.
Expected outcome: The issue is identified as pending transmission, failed transmission, rejected message, or LIS-side failure.

steps.title: Restart Only in a Controlled Order
steps.instructions:
If approved by laboratory staff and no specimens are in process, restart the affected software or workstation using the site-approved process.
If the analyzer must be restarted, confirm all samples are complete or removed first.
Expected outcome: A temporary software or connection lockup clears without interrupting patient testing.

steps.title: Retest Communication With a Non-Patient or Approved Test Record
steps.instructions:
Have laboratory staff use an approved QC, test, or facility-approved communication check process.
Do not create or transmit fake patient results unless that is part of the approved lab/IT procedure.
Expected outcome: Communication is verified safely without creating inaccurate patient data.

steps.title: Check With LIS, Middleware, or IT Support
steps.instructions:
If the IPU appears functional and locally connected, contact LIS/middleware/IT support to confirm interface status, port availability, message rejection logs, and network access.
Expected outcome: External LIS, middleware, firewall, or network causes are ruled in or ruled out.

steps.title: Return to Service Only After Verified Transmission
steps.instructions:
Confirm with laboratory staff that expected results or worklists are flowing correctly before returning the UF-1000i to normal use.
Expected outcome: The device is not returned to service until result communication is verified.

steps.title: Stop if Communication Remains Unresolved
steps.instructions:
If cabling, power, workstation status, network connection, software status, and LIS/middleware availability have been checked and the issue persists, do not continue repeated restarts.
Expected outcome: The issue is escalated appropriately instead of risking data loss or delayed results.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported the Sysmex UF-1000i results were visible on the IPU but were not transmitting to the LIS.
CCR.Cause: Network and power checks were normal, but results were held in the IPU transmission queue and middleware connectivity required LIS review.
CCR.Resolution: Verified analyzer and IPU operation, checked communication cabling and network link, documented affected result queue status, removed device from routine use, and escalated to LIS/vendor support.

helpfulDetails: Exact communication error or host message displayed
Whether results were missing, delayed, rejected, or stuck in queue
Whether worklist download was affected
Analyzer-to-IPU communication status
IPU-to-LIS or middleware communication status
Network jack and cable checked
Link lights present or absent
IPU software responsive or frozen
Any recent LIS, middleware, IT, or analyzer changes
Sample IDs or QC records affected, if allowed by policy
Whether other lab instruments were communicating normally
Restart actions performed and order used
Final device status: returned to service, monitored, or out of service

title: Sysmex UF-5000 Urine Analyzer - Sample Aspiration / Probe Clog
description: Troubleshooting UF-5000 sample aspiration errors, probe clogs, low sample pickup, bubbles, or abnormal flow before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the UF-5000 is not actively aspirating or processing patient specimens before opening covers, handling racks, checking sample areas, or initiating cleaning. Expected outcome: No patient specimen is interrupted, lost, or resulted from a device with an unresolved aspiration issue.

steps.title: Stop Additional Sample Processing
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff not to load additional racks or specimens until the aspiration issue is understood. Confirm whether any specimen was in process when the error occurred. Expected outcome: Incomplete or questionable urine results are held, repeated, or reviewed according to laboratory policy.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Check whether the issue involves sample aspiration error, probe clog message, low sample volume, bubbles in tubing, missed sample pickup, repeated reruns, or abnormal particle counts after aspiration. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to pickup, sample quality, tubing, reagent flow, or internal fluidics.

steps.title: Check the Sample Tube and Rack Position
steps.instructions: Verify the urine tube is seated correctly in the rack, the rack is the correct type, and the specimen tube is not tilted, cracked, overfilled, underfilled, or capped incorrectly. Expected outcome: The probe can reach the specimen properly without obstruction or misalignment. If this resolves the issue, return the analyzer to service after acceptable checks and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Specimen Quality
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff whether the sample contains heavy sediment, mucus, clots, crystals, foam, or insufficient volume. Poor sample quality can mimic an aspiration fault or clog the sampling path. Expected outcome: Problematic specimens are remixed, recollected, diluted, or handled per laboratory procedure before retesting. If the issue is sample-related and repeat testing passes, stop.

steps.title: Check for Visible Obstruction Around the Sampling Area
steps.instructions: With the analyzer safe and idle, inspect user-accessible sample loading and aspiration areas for spilled urine, dried residue, rack debris, tube fragments, labels, or anything blocking normal probe access. Expected outcome: No external obstruction prevents probe movement or sample pickup. If debris is safely removed and operation returns to normal, stop.

steps.title: Verify Probe Movement Is Not Being Blocked Externally
steps.instructions: Observe startup or user-permitted operational checks only if safe. Confirm the probe area is clear and that covers, racks, or tube labels are not interfering with normal motion. Expected outcome: The probe can move through its expected path without hitting an external obstruction.

steps.title: Check Reagent and Sheath Fluid Status
steps.instructions: Verify all required fluids are present, correctly installed, not empty, not expired, and not kinked or restricted at the container connections. Aspiration issues may appear when the analyzer cannot maintain proper fluid flow. Expected outcome: Fluid supply conditions are normal and no external container issue is contributing to the fault.

steps.title: Check Waste Container and Drain Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the waste container is not full, the cap or tubing is seated correctly, and the waste line is not kinked, pinched, or elevated in a way that restricts drainage. Expected outcome: Waste flow is not backing up into the fluid path. If correcting the waste setup clears the error, stop after successful testing.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible Tubing for Kinks, Air, or Leaks
steps.instructions: Look at user-accessible fluid lines for pinching, disconnection, visible bubbles, crystallized residue, leaks, or tubing caught behind containers or panels. Do not remove internal covers or disassemble the fluidics system. Expected outcome: External tubing is properly routed and intact.

steps.title: Run Approved Cleaning or Rinse Function
steps.instructions: If available to Clinical Engineering or laboratory staff, perform the analyzer’s approved cleaning, rinse, or maintenance function for aspiration or flow-related errors. Avoid unapproved flushing, solvents, or manual probing. Expected outcome: Minor residue or sample buildup is cleared through the approved process. If cleaning resolves the issue and QC or verification passes, return the device to service and stop.

steps.title: Restart Only When Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the analyzer is idle and results are protected, perform a controlled restart according to site practice. Do not restart while specimens are actively processing or while results are pending review. Expected outcome: Temporary software or sensor state issues clear without compromising patient results.

steps.title: Run a Verification Check
steps.instructions: After any correction, have the lab run approved QC, background, control, rinse, or test material as appropriate for the site. Confirm there are no repeat aspiration errors, bubbles, abnormal counts, or unresolved flags. Expected outcome: The UF-5000 demonstrates stable sample pickup and acceptable performance before patient testing resumes.

steps.title: Review Error Pattern
steps.instructions: Determine whether the aspiration issue occurs with one specimen, one rack position, one tube type, after a specific maintenance step, or on every sample. Expected outcome: A repeatable pattern helps separate sample/rack/setup issues from likely internal probe, pump, valve, sensor, or fluidics failure.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported the Sysmex UF-5000 displayed a sample aspiration/probe clog error and failed to process urine specimens reliably.
CCR.Cause: External checks found a kinked waste line behind the waste container, restricting normal fluid drainage and contributing to aspiration errors.
CCR.Resolution: Rerouted the waste line, confirmed reagent and sheath fluid connections, ran approved rinse/cleaning function, and verified operation with acceptable QC before returning the analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the analyzer was processing patient specimens when the fault occurred
Exact aspiration, probe, clog, or flow-related message displayed
Specimen condition, including sediment, mucus, crystals, foam, or low volume
Tube type, rack position, and whether the issue followed a specific rack
Reagent and sheath fluid status
Waste container level and waste tubing condition
Visible bubbles, leaks, dried residue, or crystallization
Cleaning or rinse function performed
QC, background, or verification result after troubleshooting
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Sysmex UF-5000 Urine Analyzer - Reagent / Sheath Fluid / Waste Management Error
description: Troubleshooting UF-5000 reagent, sheath fluid, diluent, waste level, bottle detection, and fluid management errors before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Sysmex UF-5000 is not actively aspirating or processing a patient specimen before opening covers, handling fluid containers, or checking waste. Expected outcome: No specimen is interrupted, lost, or resulted during fluid system troubleshooting.

steps.title: Stop Additional Sample Processing
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff not to load additional urine specimens until the fluid management error is cleared. Confirm whether any specimens were in process when the error occurred and whether results were completed, flagged, canceled, or require repeat testing per laboratory policy. Expected outcome: Incomplete or questionable urine results are not released.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Fluid Error
steps.instructions: Review the analyzer display or workstation message with laboratory staff. Determine whether the error involves: Reagent empty; Sheath fluid empty; Diluent low; Waste full; Waste bottle not detected; Reagent bottle not detected; Fluid replacement not recognized; Air in fluid line; Unexpected fluid level warning; Repeated fluid management alarm after bottle change. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to supply, waste, bottle detection, tubing, or analyzer recognition.

steps.title: Check Reagent and Sheath Fluid Levels
steps.instructions: Inspect the installed reagent, sheath fluid, and any required fluid containers for adequate volume. Confirm the correct container is installed in the correct position and is not empty, expired, damaged, collapsed, or improperly seated. Expected outcome: The UF-5000 has the required fluids available for operation. If refilling or replacing the correct container clears the error, document the action and stop.

steps.title: Verify Waste Container Status
steps.instructions: Check whether the waste container is full, nearly full, missing, capped incorrectly, or not seated properly. If full, have the waste handled according to laboratory biohazard policy. Do not overfill, bypass, or defeat waste level detection. Expected outcome: Waste has a clear path to the proper container and the analyzer can detect safe waste capacity. If correcting the waste container clears the error, document the action and stop.

steps.title: Inspect Bottle Placement and Caps
steps.instructions: Confirm each fluid bottle, cap, pickup tube, sensor connection, and waste connection is fully seated. Look for loose caps, crossed fluid lines, pinched tubing, pickup tubes not reaching fluid, or waste tubing not routed into the waste container. Expected outcome: Fluid pickup and waste routing are physically correct.

steps.title: Check for Kinked, Pinched, or Disconnected External Tubing
steps.instructions: Inspect user-accessible tubing between the analyzer and fluid containers. Look for compression behind the analyzer, tubing caught under containers, sharp bends, dried residue, or obvious disconnection. Expected outcome: Fluid and waste lines are open, connected, and free of visible obstruction. If repositioning tubing clears the fault, document the correction and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Reagent Replacement Procedure Was Followed
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether a reagent, sheath fluid, or waste container was changed shortly before the error appeared. Confirm the container was replaced using the analyzer’s normal on-screen replacement process, not simply swapped without acknowledging the change. Expected outcome: The analyzer recognizes the new fluid status correctly.

steps.title: Check for Expired or Incorrect Fluid Containers
steps.instructions: Verify that the installed reagent or sheath fluid is the correct type for the Sysmex UF-5000 and is within the allowed expiration period. Do not substitute fluids, dilute reagents, or use unofficial containers. Expected outcome: The analyzer is using approved and valid consumables.

steps.title: Look for Air in the Fluid Path
steps.instructions: Inspect visible, user-accessible fluid lines for bubbles, empty sections, or signs the bottle ran dry. If the analyzer provides a user-level prime, refill, or fluid replacement routine, have qualified laboratory staff run the appropriate routine according to site procedure. Expected outcome: Fluid lines are restored without visible air gaps.

steps.title: Check for Leaks or Residue
steps.instructions: Look around reagent, sheath fluid, and waste areas for puddling, salt-like residue, wet tubing, drips, unusual odor, or dried fluid trails. Do not continue operating the analyzer if active leaking is present. Expected outcome: No visible fluid leak is present. If leakage is found, remove the device from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Verify Waste Drain Path Is Not Restricted
steps.instructions: If the UF-5000 uses a waste container or drain connection at your site, confirm the waste path is not blocked, kinked, elevated incorrectly, or restricted. Expected outcome: Waste fluid can leave the analyzer without backup or pressure.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If supplies, waste, tubing, and bottle placement are correct but the error remains, restart only when the analyzer is idle and laboratory staff confirm no active specimen is affected. Expected outcome: A temporary recognition or software state clears after restart. If the same fluid error returns, continue escalation.

steps.title: Run a User-Level Check or Background/QC as Required
steps.instructions: After the fluid error clears, have laboratory staff perform the required analyzer readiness check, background check, QC, or startup verification according to lab policy. Expected outcome: The analyzer proves it is ready before patient testing resumes.

steps.title: Return to Service Only After Verification
steps.instructions: Do not return the UF-5000 to patient testing solely because the message disappeared. Confirm with the lab that required checks passed and no fluid, waste, or quality flags remain. Expected outcome: The analyzer is safe and ready for clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported the Sysmex UF-5000 displayed a sheath fluid management error and would not continue urine sample processing.
CCR.Cause: Found the sheath fluid container seated incorrectly with the pickup tube not fully positioned in the container.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the sheath fluid container and pickup tube, verified waste container status, had lab perform required readiness/QC checks, and returned the UF-5000 to service after error cleared.

helpfulDetails: Exact reagent, sheath fluid, or waste error displayed
Whether patient specimens were in process
Whether results were held, repeated, or released
Reagent and sheath fluid levels checked
Waste container emptied or reseated
Bottle caps and pickup tubes checked
Tubing inspected for kinks or disconnection
Air bubbles or empty line sections observed
Leak, residue, odor, or wet area present
User-level prime/refill routine performed
QC, background, or readiness check result
Final device status

title: Sysmex UF-5000 Urine Analyzer - FLOW CELL / DETECTOR BACKGROUND ABNORMALITY
description: Troubleshooting UF-5000 flow cell, detector background, carryover, debris, abnormal scatter, or background count concerns.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the UF-5000 is not actively processing patient specimens before pausing the analyzer, checking reagents, inspecting waste, or initiating cleaning. If urine testing must continue, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime process. Expected outcome: Patient results are protected while the background abnormality is investigated.

steps.title: Stop Reporting Questionable Results
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff whether patient specimens were processed while the flow cell or detector background abnormality was present. Confirm whether results were flagged, held, repeated, or reviewed according to laboratory policy. Expected outcome: Potentially unreliable urine sediment or particle results are not released without proper review.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Alarm or Behavior
steps.instructions: Review the analyzer screen with laboratory staff and identify the specific background, detector, flow cell, scattergram, or QC-related message. Check whether the issue is constant, intermittent, after startup, after reagent replacement, after cleaning, or only during specific sample types. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to background contamination, reagent/fluid issue, sample carryover, detector concern, or analyzer startup condition.

steps.title: Check Reagent and Diluent Status
steps.instructions: Verify that Sysmex-approved reagents, diluents, sheath fluid, cleaner, and waste containers are installed correctly and are not empty, expired, contaminated, or incorrectly connected. Confirm that caps, tubing connections, and pickup lines are seated correctly. Expected outcome: The analyzer has proper fluid supply and waste handling. If this resolves the abnormal background condition, document the reagent or container issue and stop.

steps.title: Inspect for Air Bubbles or Fluid Supply Interruption
steps.instructions: Look for visible air in accessible tubing, poor reagent draw, loose container connections, kinked lines, or recently replaced fluid containers that may not have been fully seated. Air or interrupted fluid flow can affect detector background and flow cell readings. Expected outcome: Fluid paths appear stable, without obvious air, leaks, or loose connections. If reseating an external container or line resolves the issue, stop and document the correction.

steps.title: Check Waste Container and Drain Condition
steps.instructions: Verify the waste container is not full and that the waste line is not kinked, pinched, elevated incorrectly, or blocked at an accessible point. Poor waste evacuation can contribute to flow issues, contamination, or abnormal background readings. Expected outcome: Waste can drain properly and is not causing backpressure or fluid handling errors. If correcting the waste setup clears the abnormality, stop.

steps.title: Review Recent Maintenance and Cleaning History
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff when the last routine cleaning, shutdown cleaning, background check, QC, or maintenance cycle was completed. Confirm whether the issue started after skipped cleaning, reagent change, heavy workload, prolonged idle time, or analyzer restart. Expected outcome: A maintenance-related cause is identified or ruled out.

steps.title: Run the Manufacturer-Approved Cleaning or Rinse Cycle
steps.instructions: Have trained laboratory staff perform the appropriate user-level cleaning, rinse, or background recovery process from the analyzer menu. Do not perform internal disassembly or manual flow cell cleaning unless authorized by the manufacturer’s service procedure. Expected outcome: Debris, carryover, or residual particles are cleared from the flow path. If the background returns to acceptable limits after cleaning, document the cleaning action and final status.

steps.title: Repeat Background Check or QC as Directed by Lab Policy
steps.instructions: After cleaning or correcting an external issue, have the lab repeat the background check, QC, or analyzer readiness check required by their procedure. Clinical Engineering should not return the device to service based only on the absence of an alarm if required QC has not passed. Expected outcome: Background and QC are acceptable before patient testing resumes. If QC and background are acceptable, return the device to service and stop.

steps.title: Check for Carryover or Problem Sample Pattern
steps.instructions: Ask whether the abnormality occurred after visibly turbid, bloody, highly concentrated, or particulate-heavy urine specimens. Confirm whether the problem clears after cleaning or returns immediately with normal samples. Expected outcome: Sample-related contamination or carryover is identified as a possible external contributor.

steps.title: Inspect Accessible Sample Area for Spills or Residue
steps.instructions: Check the rack area, sample aspiration area, and accessible covers for dried urine, spills, crystals, residue, or debris. Do not reach into moving areas or remove covers beyond normal user-accessible panels. Expected outcome: No external contamination is contributing to repeated flow or background errors. If cleaning accessible residue resolves the issue, document and stop.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the analyzer is idle and results are protected, perform a normal software-controlled restart or power cycle according to local procedure. Avoid repeated restarts if the same background abnormality returns. Expected outcome: Temporary software or initialization issues are cleared. If the fault clears and QC/background checks pass, return the analyzer to service.

steps.title: Evaluate for Recurring Internal Failure Indicators
steps.instructions: If the background abnormality returns after proper fluids, waste setup, user-level cleaning, QC, and restart, suspect an internal flow cell, detector, fluidics, optical, or contamination issue. Expected outcome: External and user-accessible causes have been ruled out.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported a UF-5000 flow cell / detector background abnormality during urine sediment analysis.
CCR.Cause: Verified analyzer was idle, checked reagent and waste setup, found no external fluid container issue, and abnormal background persisted after user-level cleaning and repeat checks.
CCR.Resolution: Removed UF-5000 from service, labeled Out of Service, notified laboratory leadership, and escalated for vendor service evaluation of the flow cell, detector, or internal fluidics.

helpfulDetails: Exact alarm or message displayed
Whether issue occurred at startup, during QC, or during patient testing
Reagent, diluent, cleaner, and sheath fluid status
Waste container level and drain condition
Whether containers or tubing were recently changed
Background check result
QC result before and after troubleshooting
Whether user-level cleaning was performed
Presence of air bubbles, leaks, residue, or spills
Whether abnormality followed a turbid or high-particulate sample
Final device status

title: Sysmex UF-5000 Urine Analyzer - QC failure / scattergram or particle count abnormality
description: Troubleshooting UF-5000 QC failures, abnormal scattergrams, questionable particle counts, reagent issues, sample handling, or external causes before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the UF-5000 is not actively processing patient specimens before troubleshooting QC, reagents, fluidics, or workstation review screens. If urine testing must continue, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime process. Expected outcome: Patient results are not released from an analyzer with unresolved QC or particle count concerns.

steps.title: Stop Reporting Questionable Results
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff whether patient specimens were processed after the QC failure, abnormal scattergram, or unexpected particle count appeared. Confirm whether affected results were held, repeated, manually reviewed, or verified per laboratory policy. Expected outcome: Questionable urine sediment or particle results are controlled before release.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Determine whether the issue involves: QC out of range; Repeated QC failure on one level; Both QC levels failing; Abnormal scattergram pattern; Unexpected RBC, WBC, epithelial cell, cast, crystal, yeast, bacteria, or conductivity-related results; Increased review flags; Background counts or carryover concerns; Reagent, sheath, or waste warning; Recent reagent, control lot, calibration, or maintenance change. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to QC material, reagent condition, sample handling, fluidics, optical detection, or analyzer performance.

steps.title: Check QC Material Handling
steps.instructions: Verify with laboratory staff that the correct QC material, level, lot number, expiration date, storage condition, and mixing method were used. Confirm the QC was allowed to reach the required operating condition if refrigerated. Expected outcome: A failed QC caused by incorrect, expired, poorly mixed, or temperature-affected control material is identified. If correcting the QC material resolves the issue, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Verify QC Lot and Target Setup
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff confirm the active QC lot, assigned values, ranges, and level selection in the UF-5000 software or connected data system. A lot mismatch can create an apparent QC failure even when the analyzer is functioning correctly. Expected outcome: The QC result is being compared against the correct control lot and expected range. If correcting the lot setup resolves the issue, document the correction and stop.

steps.title: Repeat QC Using Fresh, Properly Mixed Material
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff to repeat the failed QC level using fresh control material prepared according to laboratory procedure. Avoid repeated troubleshooting using the same questionable aliquot. Expected outcome: A one-time handling, mixing, or aliquot issue is ruled in or ruled out. If repeat QC passes and the scattergram appears normal, return the analyzer to service per lab policy and stop.

steps.title: Check Reagent and Sheath Fluid Status
steps.instructions: Inspect user-accessible reagent, diluent, sheath, stain, and waste status indicators. Confirm containers are not empty, expired, incorrectly installed, kinked, or recently changed without proper system recognition. Expected outcome: The analyzer has the correct fluids available for stable particle detection and flow cytometry performance. If replacing or reseating an external reagent container resolves the issue, document it and stop.

steps.title: Inspect for Air, Bubbles, or Fluid Path Supply Issues
steps.instructions: Look for visible air bubbles, loose bottle caps, collapsed tubing, pinched external lines, or disconnected reagent/waste tubing in user-accessible areas. Air or inconsistent fluid delivery can affect scattergram shape and particle counts. Expected outcome: External fluid supply problems are corrected before assuming analyzer failure.

steps.title: Check Waste Container and Drain Condition
steps.instructions: Confirm the waste container is not full and that drain tubing is not kinked, blocked, elevated incorrectly, or leaking. Poor waste handling can interfere with stable sample flow and trigger abnormal analyzer behavior. Expected outcome: Waste restrictions are ruled out as a cause of QC or scattergram abnormalities.

steps.title: Review Recent Maintenance or Cleaning Activity
steps.instructions: Ask whether cleaning, shutdown, reagent replacement, flow cell cleaning, background check, or other routine maintenance was recently performed. Confirm that the maintenance sequence completed normally and no errors were left active. Expected outcome: The issue is linked to an incomplete cleaning, missed prime, reagent change, or post-maintenance recovery problem if present.

steps.title: Run Approved Cleaning or Prime Functions Only
steps.instructions: If the analyzer indicates possible contamination, background count, clog, or fluid instability, have laboratory staff run only the manufacturer-approved user-level cleaning, rinse, or prime functions. Do not perform internal disassembly, flow cell removal, or board-level troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Minor contamination, bubbles, or fluid instability may clear through approved user-level maintenance. If QC passes after cleaning and scattergrams normalize, document the action and stop.

steps.title: Check Sample and Control Aspiration Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the sample probe or aspiration area is free of visible obstruction, dried residue, tube positioning problems, or incorrect sample volume. Make sure control material is presented in the correct tube type or rack position used by the lab. Expected outcome: Poor aspiration or incorrect presentation is ruled out as a cause of abnormal particle counts.

steps.title: Review Scattergram Behavior With Laboratory Staff
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the scattergram abnormality is limited to QC material, patient specimens, one particle channel, or all particle populations. A single QC level failure points more toward material or lot issues, while broad scattergram distortion may suggest fluidic, optical, or internal analyzer problems. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is better understood before escalation.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or External Interference
steps.instructions: Verify the analyzer is on a stable surface, not exposed to excessive vibration, direct heat, liquid spills, or recent movement. Confirm power is stable and the analyzer is connected to the proper outlet or UPS if required by facility practice. Expected outcome: External environmental causes are ruled out.

steps.title: Restart Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no patient specimens are running and results are protected, perform a controlled restart of the analyzer and workstation if recommended by site procedure. Allow the UF-5000 to complete startup checks before repeating QC. Expected outcome: Temporary software or communication-related abnormalities are cleared.

steps.title: Repeat QC and Confirm Result Acceptability
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff rerun the required QC levels after external checks, reagent verification, cleaning, or restart. Confirm whether QC passes, scattergrams appear acceptable, and particle counts return to expected behavior. Expected outcome: The analyzer is either confirmed acceptable for use or remains failed and requires escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported repeated UF-5000 QC failure with abnormal scattergram appearance and unexpected particle count results.
CCR.Cause: Verified QC lot setup and reagent status; found user-accessible sheath fluid line not fully seated after recent reagent replacement.
CCR.Resolution: Reseated the external sheath fluid connection, had staff run approved prime/cleaning sequence, repeated QC successfully, and returned the UF-5000 to service per lab approval.

helpfulDetails: QC level or levels affected
QC lot number and expiration checked
Control material storage and mixing verified
Reagent and sheath fluid status checked
Waste container or drain condition checked
Scattergram abnormality observed
Particle categories affected
Background count or carryover concern
Approved cleaning or prime performed
Error messages or flags displayed
Whether patient results were held or repeated
Final QC status
Final device status

title: Sysmex UF-5000 Urine Analyzer - Host / Middleware / LIS Communication Failure
description: Troubleshooting UF-5000 result transmission, host connection, middleware, worklist, or LIS communication failures before escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Sysmex UF-5000 is not actively processing patient urine specimens before restarting the analyzer, workstation, middleware connection, or network equipment. If testing must continue, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime process. Expected outcome: Patient testing and result reporting are protected during communication troubleshooting.

steps.title: Protect Result Integrity
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff whether any patient samples were processed while the communication failure was present. Confirm whether results are stored locally, pending transmission, manually entered, rejected by middleware, or missing from the LIS. Expected outcome: No urine results are lost, duplicated, or released without verification.

steps.title: Confirm the Exact Communication Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they are seeing. Determine whether the issue involves: Results not reaching the LIS; Orders or worklists not downloading; Middleware not receiving data; UF-5000 showing host offline; Results stuck in the analyzer or workstation; Barcode/sample ID mismatch after transmission; Intermittent communication delays. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to analyzer, workstation, middleware, LIS, or network communication.

steps.title: Check Analyzer Status and Error Messages
steps.instructions: Review the UF-5000 screen for communication alarms, host connection status, pending result queues, or system messages. Do not clear messages until they are documented. Expected outcome: The displayed fault gives direction for the next step and supports accurate work order documentation.

steps.title: Verify the Analyzer Is Ready and Not in an Error State
steps.instructions: Confirm the UF-5000 is powered on, initialized, not paused, and not held in an operational alarm that could prevent result transfer. Resolve any obvious user-accessible issue first, such as a stopped run, full waste, reagent issue, sample rack interruption, or analyzer not ready condition. Expected outcome: Communication troubleshooting is not confused with a general analyzer operating fault. If this restores host communication, confirm result transfer with the lab and stop.

steps.title: Check Physical Network Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect the Ethernet cable at the UF-5000, workstation or data manager, network jack, and any connected interface device. Look for loose connectors, damaged cable ends, unplugged patch cables, or network switch link lights that are off. Expected outcome: The analyzer and host pathway have a secure physical connection. If a loose cable is found and communication restores, verify pending results transmit correctly and stop.

steps.title: Check for Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether anything changed before the failure, including analyzer relocation, workstation replacement, LIS update, middleware update, network maintenance, IP change, password change, or power outage. Expected outcome: Recent changes help identify whether the failure is equipment-related, network-related, or interface-related.

steps.title: Verify Network Jack and Cable Function
steps.instructions: If allowed by facility policy, test the wall jack or swap with a known-good Ethernet cable. Do not move the analyzer to an unapproved network port unless IT or LIS support confirms it is configured for that device. Expected outcome: A bad patch cable or inactive network jack is ruled out. If a cable or jack issue is corrected and communication restores, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Workstation or Data Manager Status
steps.instructions: If the UF-5000 communicates through a workstation, data manager, or middleware interface, confirm that system is powered on, logged in as required, not frozen, and not showing database, interface, or service errors. Expected outcome: The receiving system is available to accept analyzer data.

steps.title: Check Middleware and LIS Availability
steps.instructions: Ask the lab or LIS team whether other analyzers are also having result transmission problems. If multiple instruments are affected, the issue is likely middleware, LIS, network, or interface-engine related rather than a UF-5000 hardware issue. Expected outcome: The scope of the failure is understood before unnecessary analyzer repair is pursued.

steps.title: Review Host Connection Settings Without Changing Them
steps.instructions: Compare visible host or communication settings to the facility’s known configuration if that information is available. Check for obvious mismatch concerns such as wrong host destination, disabled communication, incorrect port selection, or connection mode changed by mistake. Do not alter IP, port, protocol, or interface settings unless authorized by the lab, LIS, IT, or vendor support. Expected outcome: Configuration drift is identified without creating a larger interface issue.

steps.title: Check Pending Result Queue or Transmission Status
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff confirm whether results are queued, held, manually reviewed, or rejected before retransmission. Releasing or retransmitting patient results should follow laboratory policy to avoid duplicate or incorrect reporting. Expected outcome: Stored UF-5000 results are handled safely and consistently.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart Only When Safe
steps.instructions: If approved by the lab and no patient specimens are actively running, restart the affected communication path in a controlled order. This may include the UF-5000 interface function, workstation/data manager software, or analyzer restart depending on site policy. Avoid repeated power cycling. Expected outcome: A temporary software or session fault clears without disrupting patient testing. If communication restores, confirm successful transmission of a known test or pending result and stop.

steps.title: Coordinate With LIS, IT, or Middleware Support
steps.instructions: If the UF-5000 appears operational and physically connected but results still do not transmit, involve LIS, IT, or middleware support. Ask them to verify network reachability, interface engine status, middleware queues, rejected messages, and LIS-side errors. Expected outcome: The issue is escalated to the correct support group when the analyzer itself is not the clear failure point.

steps.title: Document Final Operational Status
steps.instructions: Before returning the UF-5000 to service, confirm with laboratory staff whether host communication is restored, pending results transmitted, and any affected specimens were reviewed under lab policy. Expected outcome: The analyzer is returned to service only after result transfer and patient-result integrity are verified.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory reported the Sysmex UF-5000 was processing urine samples but results were not transmitting to the LIS.
CCR.Cause: Ethernet connection and analyzer status were verified; results were found pending in the middleware queue after a host communication interruption.
CCR.Resolution: Confirmed analyzer was operational, coordinated with LIS support to restore the interface connection, verified pending UF-5000 results transmitted, and returned the analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Analyzer host or communication message displayed
Whether other lab analyzers were affected
Whether results were stored locally or pending in middleware
Network cable and wall jack checked
Link lights present or absent
Workstation or data manager status
Middleware queue or rejection behavior
LIS downtime or interface-engine status
Whether any patient results required manual review or retransmission
Final device status: returned to service, monitored, or removed from service
